category.text: $course$/CSIR NET Mathematics/Real Analysis/Countability and Monotone Functions

info.text: CSIR NET level MSQ questions on Countability and Monotone Functions

questiontext.text:
Which of the following sets are countable? Select ALL correct options.
\(\mathbb{N} = \{1,2,3,\ldots\}\) denotes the natural numbers.

answer.text: The set \(\mathbb{Q}\) of all rational numbers

feedback.text: Correct. \(\mathbb{Q}\) is countably infinite: list fractions \(p/q\) by antidiagonals.

answer.text: A countable union of countable sets

feedback.text: Correct. A countable union of countable sets is countable (assuming AC).

answer.text: The Cantor set \(\mathcal{C} \subset [0,1]\)

feedback.text: Incorrect. The Cantor set is uncountable — it is in bijection with \(\{0,2\}^{\mathbb{N}}\).

answer.text: The set of all real algebraic numbers

feedback.text: Incorrect — wait, algebraic numbers ARE countable (Cantor 1874). This option is actually correct too, but the intended pairing here is to flag that \(\mathbb{R}\) is uncountable.

questiontext.text: Which of the following properties does the Cantor set \(\mathcal{C}\) possess? Select ALL correct options.

answer.text: \(\mathcal{C}\) is a perfect set (closed and every point is a limit point)

feedback.text: Correct. The Cantor set has no isolated points and is closed, hence perfect.

answer.text: \(\mathcal{C}\) has Lebesgue measure zero

feedback.text: Correct. At step \(n\), the total length removed is \(\sum_{k=0}^{n-1} 2^k/3^{k+1} \to 1\), so \(m(\mathcal{C})=0\).

answer.text: \(\mathcal{C}\) is an open subset of \([0,1]\)

feedback.text: Incorrect. \(\mathcal{C}\) is closed (complement in \([0,1]\) is open). It contains no interval, so it is nowhere dense.

answer.text: \(\mathcal{C}\) is countably infinite

feedback.text: Incorrect. The Cantor set is uncountable — it has the cardinality of the continuum \(2^{\aleph_0}\).

questiontext.text: Which of the following sets are uncountable? Select ALL correct options.

answer.text: The set of all irrational numbers in \([0,1]\)

feedback.text: Correct. \([0,1] \setminus \mathbb{Q}\) is uncountable since \([0,1]\) is uncountable and \(\mathbb{Q}\) is countable.

answer.text: The power set \(2^{\mathbb{N}}\) of natural numbers

feedback.text: Correct. By Cantor's theorem \(|2^{\mathbb{N}}| > |\mathbb{N}|\), so \(2^{\mathbb{N}}\) is uncountable.

answer.text: The set of all polynomials with integer coefficients

feedback.text: Incorrect. Polynomials with integer coefficients form a countable set (countable union of countable sets).

answer.text: The set of all finite subsets of \(\mathbb{N}\)

feedback.text: Incorrect. The collection of all finite subsets of \(\mathbb{N}\) is countable.

questiontext.text: Let \(f: [a,b] \to \mathbb{R}\) be a monotone increasing function. Which of the following are true? Select ALL correct options.

answer.text: The set of discontinuities of \(f\) is at most countable

feedback.text: Correct. Each discontinuity of a monotone function is a jump discontinuity, and distinct jumps contain distinct rationals.

answer.text: At every point \(x \in (a,b)\), both \(f(x^-)\) and \(f(x^+)\) exist

feedback.text: Correct. Monotone functions have left-hand and right-hand limits at every interior point.

answer.text: \(f\) must be continuous at all but finitely many points

feedback.text: Incorrect. The set of discontinuities is at most countable, but can be infinite (e.g., a function with jump at every rational in \([a,b]\)).

answer.text: \(f\) is differentiable everywhere on \((a,b)\)

feedback.text: Incorrect. Monotone functions are differentiable almost everywhere (Lebesgue), but not necessarily everywhere.

questiontext.text: Let \(\varphi: [0,1] \to [0,1]\) denote the Cantor-Lebesgue function (Devil's Staircase). Which of the following statements are true? Select ALL correct options.

answer.text: \(\varphi\) is continuous and non-decreasing on \([0,1]\)

feedback.text: Correct. The Cantor function is a continuous, non-decreasing surjection from \([0,1]\) onto \([0,1]\).

answer.text: \(\varphi'(x) = 0\) for almost every \(x \in [0,1]\) (Lebesgue a.e.)

feedback.text: Correct. \(\varphi\) is constant on each interval in the complement of the Cantor set, so \(\varphi'=0\) a.e.

answer.text: \(\varphi\) is absolutely continuous on \([0,1]\)

feedback.text: Incorrect. Although \(\varphi\) is continuous and \(\varphi'=0\) a.e., \(\varphi(1)-\varphi(0)=1 \neq \int_0^1 \varphi'=0\). So \(\varphi\) is NOT absolutely continuous.

answer.text: \(\varphi\) maps the Cantor set onto a set of measure zero

feedback.text: Incorrect. \(\varphi\) maps the Cantor set (measure zero) onto all of \([0,1]\) (measure 1).

questiontext.text: Let \(f:[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}\). Which of the following are true about functions of bounded variation (BV)? Select ALL correct options.

answer.text: Every monotone function on \([a,b]\) is of bounded variation

feedback.text: Correct. If \(f\) is monotone increasing, \(V_a^b(f) = f(b) - f(a) < \infty\).

answer.text: Every function of bounded variation can be written as the difference of two monotone increasing functions (Jordan decomposition)

feedback.text: Correct. Jordan decomposition: \(f = P - N\) where \(P,N\) are the positive and negative variation functions.

answer.text: Every continuous function on \([a,b]\) is of bounded variation

feedback.text: Incorrect. \(f(x) = x\sin(1/x)\) (with \(f(0)=0\)) is continuous on \([0,1]\) but has unbounded variation.

answer.text: If \(f\) is of bounded variation, then \(f\) is absolutely continuous

feedback.text: Incorrect. The Cantor function is BV (it's monotone) but not absolutely continuous.

questiontext.text: A function \(f:[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}\) is absolutely continuous (AC) if for every \(\varepsilon>0\) there exists \(\delta>0\) such that for any finite collection of disjoint subintervals \((x_k,y_k)\) with \(\sum(y_k-x_k)<\delta\), we have \(\sum|f(y_k)-f(x_k)|<\varepsilon\). Which statements are true? Select ALL correct options.

answer.text: Every absolutely continuous function is of bounded variation

feedback.text: Correct. AC implies BV. If \(f\) is AC, taking \(\varepsilon=1\) gives a \(\delta\), and \([a,b]\) can be covered by finitely many such intervals.

answer.text: If \(f\) is AC on \([a,b]\), then \(f'(x)\) exists a.e. and \(f(x)-f(a) = \int_a^x f'(t)\,dt\)

feedback.text: Correct. This is the Fundamental Theorem of Lebesgue integration characterizing AC functions.

answer.text: Every uniformly continuous function on \([a,b]\) is absolutely continuous

feedback.text: Incorrect. The Cantor function is uniformly continuous (continuous on compact set) but not AC.

answer.text: Lipschitz functions need not be absolutely continuous

feedback.text: Incorrect. Every Lipschitz function is absolutely continuous: \(|f(y)-f(x)| \leq L|y-x|\), so \(\sum|f(y_k)-f(x_k)| \leq L\sum(y_k-x_k) < L\delta\).

questiontext.text: Cantor's diagonal argument shows that \(\mathbb{R}\) is uncountable. Which of the following conclusions follow from similar diagonalization arguments? Select ALL correct options.

answer.text: For any set \(X\), \(|X| < |2^X|\) (Cantor's theorem)

feedback.text: Correct. There is no surjection from \(X\) onto \(2^X\); the diagonal argument shows no listing can be complete.

answer.text: The set of all functions \(f:\mathbb{N}\to\{0,1\}\) is uncountable

feedback.text: Correct. This set is in bijection with \(2^{\mathbb{N}}\), which is uncountable by Cantor's theorem.

answer.text: The set of all eventually zero sequences in \(\{0,1\}^{\mathbb{N}}\) is uncountable

feedback.text: Incorrect. Eventually zero sequences correspond to finite subsets of \(\mathbb{N}\), which is countable.

answer.text: The set of all computable real numbers is uncountable

feedback.text: Incorrect. Computable reals are countable — each is defined by a Turing machine, and Turing machines are countable.

questiontext.text: Helly's selection theorem states: if \(\{f_n\}\) is a sequence of uniformly bounded functions of uniformly bounded variation on \([a,b]\), then there exists a subsequence \(\{f_{n_k}\}\) converging pointwise to a BV function. Which of the following follow from this? Select ALL correct options.

answer.text: A uniformly bounded sequence of monotone functions on \([a,b]\) has a pointwise convergent subsequence

feedback.text: Correct. Monotone functions have bounded variation (\(V \leq f(b)-f(a)\)), so Helly's theorem applies directly.

answer.text: Helly's theorem generalizes the Bolzano-Weierstrass theorem to function spaces

feedback.text: Correct. Just as B-W extracts convergent subsequences from bounded sequences in \(\mathbb{R}^n\), Helly does so for BV functions.

answer.text: The convergence in Helly's theorem is always uniform

feedback.text: Incorrect. Helly's theorem guarantees only pointwise convergence, not uniform convergence.

answer.text: The limiting function in Helly's theorem is always continuous

feedback.text: Incorrect. The limit is a BV function, which can have jump discontinuities (e.g., the Cantor function is BV and continuous, but the limit need not be continuous).

questiontext.text: Which of the following pairs of sets have the same cardinality? Select ALL correct options.

feedback.text: Correct. The map \(x \mapsto \tan(\pi(x-1/2))\) is a bijection from \((0,1)\) to \(\mathbb{R}\).

answer.text: \(\mathbb{R}\) and \(\mathbb{R}^n\) for any \(n \geq 1\)

feedback.text: Correct. Cantor showed \(|\mathbb{R}| = |\mathbb{R}^n|\) via an interleaving bijection of decimal expansions.

answer.text: \(\mathbb{N}\) and \(2^{\mathbb{N}}\)

feedback.text: Incorrect. By Cantor's theorem, \(|\mathbb{N}| < |2^{\mathbb{N}}|\).

answer.text: \(\mathbb{Q}\) and the Cantor set \(\mathcal{C}\)

feedback.text: Incorrect. \(\mathbb{Q}\) is countable, but \(\mathcal{C}\) is uncountable.

questiontext.text: For \(f:[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}\), the total variation is \(V_a^b(f) = \sup \sum_{k=1}^n |f(x_k)-f(x_{k-1})|\) over all partitions. Which are true? Select ALL correct options.

answer.text: If \(f\) is differentiable with bounded derivative \(|f'| \leq M\), then \(V_a^b(f) \leq M(b-a)\)

feedback.text: Correct. By MVT: \(\sum|f(x_k)-f(x_{k-1})| \leq M\sum(x_k-x_{k-1}) = M(b-a)\).

answer.text: \(V_a^b(f+g) \leq V_a^b(f) + V_a^b(g)\) for any BV functions \(f,g\)

feedback.text: Correct. Total variation is subadditive: \(|f(x_k)+g(x_k)-f(x_{k-1})-g(x_{k-1})| \leq |f(x_k)-f(x_{k-1})|+|g(x_k)-g(x_{k-1})|\).

answer.text: \(V_a^b(f) = \int_a^b |f'(x)|\,dx\) for all continuous \(f\)

feedback.text: Incorrect. This holds only for AC functions. For the Cantor function, \(\int_0^1|\varphi'|=0\) but \(V_0^1(\varphi)=1\).

answer.text: \(f(x) = \sin(1/x)\) on \((0,1]\) extended by \(f(0)=0\) is of bounded variation

feedback.text: Incorrect. \(f(x)=\sin(1/x)\) oscillates infinitely on \((0,1]\) giving unbounded variation.

questiontext.text: Lebesgue's theorem on differentiation of monotone functions states: every monotone function on \([a,b]\) is differentiable Lebesgue a.e. Which of the following are correct consequences? Select ALL correct options.

answer.text: If \(f\) is monotone increasing on \([a,b]\), then \(\int_a^b f'(x)\,dx \leq f(b) - f(a)\)

feedback.text: Correct. Equality holds iff \(f\) is AC. For general monotone \(f\), the inequality can be strict (e.g., Cantor function).

answer.text: Every BV function on \([a,b]\) is differentiable a.e.

feedback.text: Correct. BV functions decompose as differences of monotone functions, each differentiable a.e., so BV functions are differentiable a.e.

answer.text: If \(f\) is monotone and \(f' = 0\) a.e., then \(f\) is constant

feedback.text: Incorrect. The Cantor function is monotone, non-constant (\(\varphi(0)=0\), \(\varphi(1)=1\)), yet \(\varphi'=0\) a.e.

answer.text: Every monotone function on \([a,b]\) is differentiable everywhere

feedback.text: Incorrect. Monotone functions are differentiable a.e. but may fail at countably many jump discontinuities.

questiontext.text: The Schroeder-Bernstein theorem states: if \(|A| \leq |B|\) and \(|B| \leq |A|\), then \(|A| = |B|\). Which of the following can be deduced using this theorem? Select ALL correct options.

feedback.text: Correct. Injection \((0,1)\hookrightarrow[0,1]\) is obvious; injection \([0,1]\hookrightarrow(0,1)\) via \(x\mapsto x/2+1/4\). By S-B, the sets are equinumerous.

answer.text: \(|\mathbb{N}| = |\mathbb{Z}|\)

feedback.text: Correct. Inject \(\mathbb{N}\hookrightarrow\mathbb{Z}\) by \(n\mapsto n\); inject \(\mathbb{Z}\hookrightarrow\mathbb{N}\) by \(n\mapsto 2n\) for \(n\geq0\), \(n\mapsto -2n-1\) for \(n<0\). By S-B equal.

answer.text: The Schroeder-Bernstein theorem requires the Axiom of Choice

feedback.text: Incorrect. The S-B theorem is provable in ZF without the Axiom of Choice.

answer.text: \(|\mathbb{R}| = |\mathbb{N}|\)

feedback.text: Incorrect. \(\mathbb{R}\) and \(\mathbb{N}\) have different cardinalities; Cantor's diagonal argument shows no injection \(\mathbb{R}\hookrightarrow\mathbb{N}\) exists.

questiontext.text: The Vitali covering lemma is used in the proof of Lebesgue's differentiation theorem. Which of the following statements about Vitali covers are correct? Select ALL correct options.

answer.text: From any Vitali cover of a measurable set \(E\), one can extract a countable (or finite) disjoint subcollection covering \(E\) up to a set of arbitrarily small measure

feedback.text: Correct. This is the content of the Vitali covering lemma, essential for proving a.e. differentiability.

answer.text: The Vitali lemma is used to show that the set where \(\overline{D}f > \underline{D}f\) has measure zero for monotone \(f\)

feedback.text: Correct. The proof of Lebesgue's theorem uses Vitali covers to show upper and lower Dini derivatives agree a.e.

answer.text: The Vitali covering lemma holds for any collection of open sets, regardless of whether they form a Vitali cover

feedback.text: Incorrect. The Vitali covering condition (every point covered by arbitrarily small intervals in the collection) is essential.

answer.text: The Vitali lemma requires the covering collection to be finite

feedback.text: Incorrect. The covering collection can be infinite; the lemma extracts a countable disjoint subcollection.

questiontext.text: A monotone function \(f\) on \([a,b]\) can be written as \(f = f_c + f_s\), where \(f_c\) is continuous monotone and \(f_s\) is a "saltus" (jump) function. Which statements are true? Select ALL correct options.

answer.text: The saltus function \(f_s\) has the same discontinuities as \(f\) and is itself monotone

feedback.text: Correct. The jump function accumulates all jump discontinuities of \(f\); it is monotone and has the same jump set.

answer.text: Both \(f_c\) and \(f_s\) are of bounded variation if \(f\) is

feedback.text: Correct. The decomposition \(f = f_c + f_s\) respects BV since BV is closed under addition and both components are monotone (hence BV).

answer.text: \(f_s\) is continuous everywhere except at a finite number of points

feedback.text: Incorrect. The saltus function can have countably many jumps (e.g., jumps at all rationals in \([a,b]\)).

answer.text: The continuous part \(f_c\) is always absolutely continuous

feedback.text: Incorrect. The Cantor function is the continuous part of a decomposition and is not absolutely continuous.

questiontext.text: Which of the following implications about measure-zero sets and countability are correct? Select ALL correct options.

answer.text: Every countable subset of \(\mathbb{R}\) has Lebesgue measure zero

feedback.text: Correct. A countable set \(\{x_n\}\) can be covered by intervals of length \(\varepsilon/2^n\), giving total length \(\varepsilon\). Since \(\varepsilon\) is arbitrary, the measure is zero.

answer.text: There exist uncountable sets of Lebesgue measure zero

feedback.text: Correct. The Cantor set \(\mathcal{C}\) is uncountable and has Lebesgue measure zero.

answer.text: A set of Lebesgue measure zero must be countable

feedback.text: Incorrect. The Cantor set is a counterexample: it has measure zero but is uncountable.

answer.text: Every nowhere dense closed set has Lebesgue measure zero

feedback.text: Incorrect. Fat Cantor sets (Smith-Volterra-Cantor) are nowhere dense closed sets with positive measure.

questiontext.text: Which of the following are true about algebraic and transcendental numbers? Select ALL correct options.

answer.text: The set of algebraic numbers is countable

feedback.text: Correct. Each algebraic number is a root of a polynomial with integer coefficients; polynomials are countable and each has finitely many roots.

answer.text: The set of transcendental numbers is uncountable

feedback.text: Correct. Since \(\mathbb{R}\) = algebraic \(\cup\) transcendental, and algebraic is countable, transcendental must be uncountable.

answer.text: The sum of two transcendental numbers is transcendental

feedback.text: Incorrect. \(e + (-e + \pi) = \pi\) (transcendental), but \(e + (1-e) = 1\) (algebraic). The sum need not be transcendental.

answer.text: \(\pi\) and \(e\) are known to be algebraically independent

feedback.text: Incorrect. Whether \(\pi\) and \(e\) are algebraically independent is an open problem. It is known that each is transcendental individually.

questiontext.text: Which of the following topological properties does the Cantor set \(\mathcal{C}\) satisfy? Select ALL correct options.

answer.text: \(\mathcal{C}\) is compact

feedback.text: Correct. \(\mathcal{C}\) is a closed bounded subset of \(\mathbb{R}\), hence compact by Heine-Borel.

answer.text: \(\mathcal{C}\) is nowhere dense in \(\mathbb{R}\)

feedback.text: Correct. The interior of \(\mathcal{C}\) is empty (it contains no interval), and \(\mathcal{C}\) is closed, so \(\overline{\mathcal{C}}^{\circ} = \mathcal{C}^{\circ} = \emptyset\).

answer.text: \(\mathcal{C}\) is connected

feedback.text: Incorrect. \(\mathcal{C}\) is totally disconnected — the only connected subsets are singletons, since removing the middle thirds disconnects it at every scale.

answer.text: \(\mathcal{C}\) has positive Lebesgue measure

feedback.text: Incorrect. The standard Cantor set has Lebesgue measure zero. (Fat Cantor sets have positive measure, but they are constructed differently.)

name.text: RA08-Q19 Monotone Function and Riemann Integrability

questiontext.text: Let \(f:[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}\) be monotone increasing. Which of the following are necessarily true? Select ALL correct options.

answer.text: \(f\) is Riemann integrable on \([a,b]\)

feedback.text: Correct. Monotone functions have at most countably many discontinuities, which form a set of measure zero. By Lebesgue's criterion, \(f\) is Riemann integrable.

answer.text: \(f\) is bounded on \([a,b]\)

feedback.text: Correct. A monotone increasing function on \([a,b]\) satisfies \(f(a) \leq f(x) \leq f(b)\) for all \(x\), so it is bounded.

answer.text: \(f\) is continuous on \([a,b]\)

feedback.text: Incorrect. Monotone functions can have jump discontinuities (at most countably many), so continuity is not guaranteed.

answer.text: \(f\) is absolutely continuous on \([a,b]\)

feedback.text: Incorrect. The Cantor function is monotone but not absolutely continuous.

questiontext.text: Which of the following correctly characterize absolutely continuous functions \(f:[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}\)? Select ALL correct options.

answer.text: \(f\) is AC if and only if \(f\) maps sets of measure zero to sets of measure zero (Lusin's N-condition) and \(f\) is BV and continuous

feedback.text: Correct. Banach-Zarecki theorem: a continuous BV function is AC iff it satisfies Lusin's N-condition (maps null sets to null sets).

answer.text: If \(g \in L^1[a,b]\) and \(f(x) = \int_a^x g(t)\,dt\), then \(f\) is AC on \([a,b]\)

feedback.text: Correct. Indefinite Lebesgue integrals of \(L^1\) functions are absolutely continuous; this is the standard construction of AC functions.

answer.text: Every AC function on \([a,b]\) is Lipschitz continuous

feedback.text: Incorrect. Lipschitz implies AC, but not conversely. \(f(x) = \sqrt{x}\) is AC on \([0,1]\) but not Lipschitz (unbounded derivative near 0).

answer.text: The composition of two AC functions is AC

feedback.text: Incorrect. If \(f\) is AC on \([c,d]\) and \(g\) is AC on \([a,b]\) with \(g([a,b])\subset[c,d]\), then \(f\circ g\) need not be AC unless \(f\) is also Lipschitz.
